title: Behind the Scenes - Introduction to Artificial Intelligence with Brian Yu - Chapter 1 - Playing
summary: CS50 is Harvard University's introduction to computer science course taught by David J. Malan. The "Behind the Scenes" series with Brian Yu covers artificial intelligence fundamentals, with Chapter 1 focused on game playing. The course is available through multiple platforms including edX, Harvard Extension School, Harvard Summer School, and OpenCourseWare.
writeup: This CS50 Introduction to Artificial Intelligence lecture, delivered by instructor Brian as a dress rehearsal with CS50's David Malan, builds from a definition of AI as computers that acquire knowledge and make decisions, through a survey of the field's main problem classes (games, prediction, recommendation, perception, natural language, generation, robotics), to a ground-up construction of game-playing AI using tic-tac-toe as the teaching vehicle. The through-line is that formalizing human strategy into explicit, ordered questions a computer can evaluate is both the earliest historical approach to AI and the foundation for understanding why more general search algorithms became necessary when rule lists run out.
key_points: Game-playing was one of the first serious AI research domains in the 1950s and 1960s precisely because games offer constrained, rule-governed environments with far fewer variables than the real world, making them tractable test beds for intelligent behavior.

A rule-based tic-tac-toe strategy can be encoded as a priority-ordered checklist: first check whether X can win immediately, then check whether O will win on the next move and block, with these two cases covering the obvious tactical situations.

The limitation of explicit rule-based strategy surfaces immediately on non-obvious board positions where neither player can win or must block on the next turn, motivating the need for a more general search method rather than an ever-growing list of hand-coded cases.

AI problems span six broad classes discussed in the lecture: playing games, making predictions (e.g., spam detection), analyzing data for recommendations, sensing the world (image and handwriting recognition), communicating in natural language, and generating new content or acting physically in the world.

chapters.title: Why Games Were the First AI Testbed
